team.title: {count} researchers. One mission.

founder.quote: Full Professor at the University of Granada. Co-author of the Athlete 2.0 paradigm (2012, with Greg Atkinson) and author of Cycling 3.0 (2025). Movistar Team's Performance Director for a decade.

team.closingQuote: "We come from real competition, with over 25 years of experience at the highest level and proven success, always close to the best cyclists. Are you the next one?"

publications.title: 100+ scientific articles published by the team.
publications.body: Research in high-impact journals on biomechanics, physiology, nutrition, psychology and anti-doping applied to cycling.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.q1Question: How long do you take to reply?
faq.q1Answer: We reply to every enquiry within 48 working hours. For very specific requests we may ask for extra context before drafting a proposal.
faq.q2Question: Do you work with amateur cyclists?
faq.q2Answer: Yes, though our main focus is pro cyclists and teams. We work with advanced amateurs with clear goals and real commitment.
faq.q3Question: Is there availability for new pro athletes in 2026?
faq.q3Answer: Availability is limited and confirmed case by case after an initial conversation. Tell us about your project and we'll be honest about whether we can take it on.
faq.q4Question: Do you offer one-off sessions or only ongoing programmes?
faq.q4Answer: Both: ongoing coaching programmes and one-off consultancies or assessments on specific topics.

hero.title: From Cycling 1.0 to <highlight>Cycling 3.0.</highlight>
hero.subtitle: Three paradigms that define how we understand cycling today and where we're heading.

philosophy.intro: Sport is constantly evolving. The demands of performance, science and technology advance at an unprecedented pace, and with them the need for methodologies that match. That's why we've built our work on three paradigms that define how we understand cycling today and where we're heading.

p1.subtitle: What we leave behind
p1.title: Hierarchy, intuition and secrecy.
p1.body: Cycling 1.0 was based on hierarchy, intuition and secrecy. Training depended on the coach's tacit judgement —usually a doctor—, with no structured scientific collaboration. That opacity allowed practices that crossed ethical, medical and professional limits to thrive in the shadows. The doping scandals of the 90s and 2000s were not only a moral crisis: they were a knowledge crisis.

callout.l3: That's how it's always been done.
callout.caption: Phrases that Cycling 2.0 and 3.0 firmly reject.

p2.subtitle: The cyclist takes the lead
p2.title: Data. Multidisciplinary team. Education.
p2.body1: Cycling 2.0, conceptualised by Zabala and Atkinson (2012), was both a scientific and ethical revolution. The athlete stopped being a passive subject and became the main actor in their own process. Performance decisions started to be based on reproducible data —power, heart rate variability (HRV), lactate thresholds, biomechanics…— interpreted by a multidisciplinary team.
p2.body2: This model relies on collaborative work between cyclist, coach, physiotherapist, doctor, nutritionist and psychologist. Not as a sum of parts, but as an integrated system where information flows in all directions and knowledge is shared for the good of the athlete and the whole team.

callout.text: Education: the athlete understands their own data, participates in planning and grows in autonomy. The pedagogical principle holds the same rank as any other training principle.

p2.aside: Journal of Science and Cycling — founding paradigm

p3.subtitle: The era amplified by AI
p3.title: 3.0 doesn't replace 2.0. <highlight>It amplifies it.</highlight>
p3.body1: We are at the beginning of an era amplified by artificial intelligence (AI), data-intensive and dependent on responsible governance frameworks.
p3.body2: AI introduces predictive analytics, machine learning and digital twin models into sports sciences. Well implemented, it can integrate data from multiple sensors, improve safety through early warning algorithms, support anti-doping through data intelligence, and expand athlete education through adaptive learning interfaces.

warning.text: Without solid ethical governance, AI could reverse the advances of 2.0. Algorithmic opacity, data commodification and unequal access generate new vulnerabilities.

p3.closing: That's why 3.0 must be ethics by design.

principles.title: The principles of Cycling 3.0
principles.p1: The will to improve continuously and rigorously.
principles.p2: Curiosity, learning and teaching as a permanent attitude.
principles.p5: The cyclist's active participation in their training plan.
principles.p6: Critical knowledge and use of the latest technologies and scientific advances.
principles.p7: Understanding what is being done and why.
principles.p8: Artificial Intelligence is our ally, not our replacement. We make critical, responsible and ethical use of AI that helps us raise our professional quality and be more efficient.
principles.p10: Fair play: sport without doping, with ethics by design.

quote.text: "The success of Cycling 3.0 doesn't depend on how fast we adopt new algorithms, but on the <highlight>wisdom with which we integrate them</highlight> into a culture of shared knowledge, respect and responsibility."
quote.attribution: — Mikel Zabala, 2025 · Journal of Science and Cycling

bridge.title: Who is <highlight>Cyclist 3.0</highlight>?
bridge.body: The heir to Athlete 2.0: undisputed protagonist of the process, helped by AI but with the human right to decide.
